content:
# Romans Chapter 7 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 7 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 7:1 — "binding on a person" vs. "has lordship over the human" vs. "applies only while a person is living"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the law has lordship over the human for as long a time as he lives" | Literal — kurieuei ("lords over/has lordship"); anthropos ("human") |
| LOGOS | "the law is binding on a person only as long as he lives" | Formal — "binding on" for kurieuei; "person" for anthropos |
| PNEUMA | "don't you know that the law applies only while a person is living?" | Dynamic — "applies" weakens kurieuei significantly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Kurieuei (from kurios, "lord") implies authority and dominion, not merely applicability. LOGOS's "binding on" captures the obligatory force. PNEUMA's "applies" is too neutral — the law does not merely apply; it rules.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:2 — "married woman" vs. "woman under a husband" — marriage terminology

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the married woman has been bound by law to the living husband" | Literal — he hupandros gune, "the under-a-husband woman" |
| LOGOS | "a married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "when a woman marries, the law binds her to her husband as long as he is alive" | Dynamic — restructures as temporal clause; "when a woman marries" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's temporal restructuring ("when a woman marries") shifts the emphasis from the legal status (being married) to the act of marrying. Paul's point is about the ongoing legal bond, not the wedding.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:4 — "died to the law through the body of Christ" — the analogy application

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you also were made dead to the law through the body of the Christos" | Literal — ethanatothete, "were put to death" (passive) |
| LOGOS | "you also have died to the law through the body of Christ" | Formal — "have died" simplifies the passive |
| PNEUMA | "You died to the power of the law when you died with Christ" | Dynamic — adds "the power of" and "when you died with Christ" (interpretive expansion) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET correctly identifies the passive voice ("were made dead" — someone acted on them), but LOGOS's "have died" is standard English. PNEUMA's "the power of the law" and "when you died with Christ" are interpretive additions — Paul says "through the body of Christ" (his crucified body), not "when you died with Christ."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:5 — "living in the flesh" vs. "controlled by our old nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when we were in the flesh" | Literal — en te sarki, "in the flesh" |
| LOGOS | "while we were living in the flesh" | Formal — adds "living" for context |
| PNEUMA | "When we were controlled by our old nature" | Dynamic — "old nature" for sarx; "controlled by" interprets the preposition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "In the flesh" (en te sarki) is a Pauline technical term for existence under the old Adamic regime. PNEUMA's "controlled by our old nature" interprets sarx as "old nature," which is one possible meaning but narrows the anthropological complexity of the term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:6 — "new way of the Spirit" vs. "newness of spirit" vs. "new way of living in the Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we serve in newness of spirit and not in oldness of letter" | Literal — kainoteti pneumatos / palaiotheti grammatos; lowercase "spirit" |
| LOGOS | "we serve in the new way of the Spirit and not in the old way of the written code" | Formal — capitalized "Spirit"; "written code" for gramma |
| PNEUMA | "Now we can serve God, not in the old way of obeying the letter of the law, but in the new way of living in the Spirit" | Dynamic — adds "of living" and "of obeying the letter of the law" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The capitalization of "Spirit" (linking to Romans 8) is theologically warranted — Paul is describing the new covenant reality of Spirit-empowered service. EMET's lowercase preserves ambiguity but under-determines the reference. PNEUMA's expansions add interpretive content.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:7 — "covet" vs. "coveting" — the tenth commandment

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I did not know the sin except through [the] law. For I would not have known the coveting if the law did not say, 'You shall not covet.'" | Literal — epithumia/epithumeo; "the coveting" (articular) |
| LOGOS | "I would not have known sin... I would not have known what it is to covet if the law had not said, 'You shall not covet'" | Formal — "what it is to covet" |
| PNEUMA | "it was the law that showed me my sin. I would never have known that coveting is wrong if the law had not said, 'You must not covet'" | Dynamic — "showed me my sin"; "coveting is wrong" adds moral judgment; "must not" for "shall not" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "showed me my sin" is not what Paul says — he says he would not have "known" (egno) sin. The law does not merely display sin; it enables the knowledge/experience of sin. "You must not" (PNEUMA) vs. "You shall not" (LOGOS/EMET) is a minor but real difference — "shall not" preserves the apodictic legal form of the Decalogue.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:8 — "opportunity" vs. "beachhead" — aphorme

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sin, having taken opportunity through the commandment" | Literal — aphorme, "starting point, base of operations, opportunity" |
| LOGOS | "sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment" | Formal — "seizing an opportunity" — more active verb |
| PNEUMA | "sin used this command to arouse all kinds of covetous desires within me" | Dynamic — replaces the military metaphor entirely; "arouse" for "produced" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Aphorme is a military term (base of operations), which both EMET and LOGOS render as "opportunity." LOGOS's active "seizing" better captures the aggressive agency Paul attributes to sin. PNEUMA loses the metaphor entirely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The military resonance of aphorme is noted in council notes.

---

## 7:9 — "I was once alive apart from the law" — the identity of the "I"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I was living apart from [the] law once; but when the commandment came, the sin came alive" | Literal — preserves the aorist narrative |
| LOGOS | "I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came alive and I died" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "At one time I lived without understanding the law. But when I learned the command not to covet, for instance, the power of sin came to life" | Dynamic — "without understanding" interprets "apart from"; adds "for instance"; "power of sin" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "without understanding the law" changes Paul's meaning — he does not say he lacked understanding but that he existed "apart from" (choris) the law's jurisdiction. "For instance" is an inappropriate hedge on what Paul presents as a definitive statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:14 — "of the flesh, sold under sin" vs. "all too human, a slave to sin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am fleshly, having been sold under the sin" | Literal — sarkinos eimi, pepramenos hupo ten hamartian |
| LOGOS | "I am of the flesh, sold under sin" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "The trouble is with me, for I am all too human, a slave to sin" | Dynamic — "all too human" for sarkinos; "slave" for "sold under" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Of the flesh" (sarkinos) is a technical Pauline term that should not be reduced to "all too human" (PNEUMA), which lacks the specific theological weight. "Sold under sin" (the slavery/market metaphor) is more precise than PNEUMA's generic "slave to sin."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:15 — "I do not understand my own actions" vs. "I do not know what I am working out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what I am working out, I do not understand" | Literal — ho katergazomai ou ginosko; "working out" for katergazomai |
| LOGOS | "I do not understand my own actions" | Formal — "actions" for katergazomai |
| PNEUMA | "I don't really understand myself" | Dynamic — shifts from actions to self-understanding |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul is not saying he does not understand himself (PNEUMA) but that he does not understand what his actions produce. Katergazomai means "to accomplish, to work out to completion" — Paul is baffled by the disconnect between intention and outcome.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:18 — "in my flesh" vs. "in my sinful nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there does not dwell in me — that is, in the flesh of me — [any] good" | Literal — en te sarki mou |
| LOGOS | "nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my flesh" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "nothing good lives in me, that is, in my sinful nature" | Dynamic — "sinful nature" for sarx |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in my flesh." Sarx in Paul is a complex term — it is not simply "sinful nature." Sarx refers to the whole person as oriented away from God, including but not limited to moral failure. "Sinful nature" (PNEUMA) is a common dynamic rendering but narrows the anthropological concept.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This decision applies to all occurrences of sarx in the chapter.

---

## 7:22 — "I delight in the law of God, in my inner being" vs. "I love God's law with all my heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I delight in the law of the God according to the inner human" | Literal — sunedomai to nomo tou theou kata ton eso anthropon |
| LOGOS | "I delight in the law of God, in my inner being" | Formal — "inner being" for ton eso anthropon |
| PNEUMA | "I love God's law with all my heart" | Dynamic — "love" for sunedomai; "with all my heart" for "inner being" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Sunedomai means "to rejoice with, to delight in" — a stronger and more specific verb than "love." Paul's "inner human" (eso anthropos) is a technical anthropological term for the rational/spiritual self, not simply "heart."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:23 — "another law" / "law of my mind" / "law of sin" — the four uses of nomos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I see another law in the members of me warring against the law of the mind of me and taking me captive to the law of the sin" | Literal — preserves all three uses of nomos with different qualifiers |
| LOGOS | "I see in my members another law waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin that dwells in my members" | Formal — same structure with smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "there is another power within me that is at war with my mind. This power makes me a slave to the sin that is still within me" | Dynamic — replaces "law" with "power" in all instances |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul's repeated use of nomos ("law") in different senses is deliberate — he is demonstrating that multiple "laws" (principles) compete within the human person. PNEUMA's substitution of "power" for "law" loses this rhetorical device and the theological argument about competing normative forces.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's rendering is flagged as obscuring Paul's argument.

---

## 7:24 — "Wretched man that I am!" vs. "Wretched human that I am!" vs. "Oh, what a miserable person I am!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Wretched human [that] I [am]!" | Literal — talaiporos ego anthropos |
| LOGOS | "Wretched man that I am!" | Traditional formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Oh, what a miserable person I am!" | Dynamic — "miserable" for talaiporos; adds "Oh" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Wretched man that I am!" is the classic English rendering and preserves the exclamatory force. PNEUMA's "miserable" is adequate but "wretched" (talaiporos = worn out by toil, afflicted) is more precise. The "Oh" is an unnecessary addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:24 — "body of death" vs. "this body of death" vs. "life dominated by sin and death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the body of this death" | Literal — tou somatos tou thanatou toutou |
| LOGOS | "this body of death" | Formal — "this" modifies "body" rather than "death" |
| PNEUMA | "this life that is dominated by sin and death" | Dynamic — replaces "body" with "life"; adds "sin and" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek is ambiguous (does "this" modify "body" or "death"?), but "this body of death" is the standard reading. PNEUMA's replacement of "body" with "life" loses the somatic dimension — Paul is talking about physical existence under the power of death, not just life in general.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:25 — "Thanks be to God" vs. "Thanks to the God" — Sinaiticus variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Thanks [be] to the God through Yeshua Christos the Lord of us!" | Notes Sinaiticus reads charis to theo (shorter form) |
| LOGOS | "Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Thank God! The answer is in Jesus Christ our Lord." | Dynamic — "The answer is in" is an interpretive addition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "The answer is in" adds a phrase not in the Greek — Paul simply exclaims thanks, he does not label it as "the answer."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Sinaiticus shorter reading noted.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET too literal / wooden for English | 4 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA too expansive / interpretive | 11 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA nearly identical | 12 | LOGOS rendering adopted as consensus baseline |
| sarx/"flesh" translation decision | 3 | "Flesh" retained over "sinful nature" |
| nomos/"law" translation decision | 1 | "Law" retained over "power" (v.23) |
| High agreement across all three | 3 | vv.10, 12, 16 — minimal divergence |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 7 presents the most pronounced divergence between PNEUMA and the other two translators. PNEUMA consistently replaces Paul's technical theological vocabulary with accessible paraphrases: sarx becomes "sinful nature" or "old nature" or "all too human"; nomos becomes "power"; sunedomai becomes "love"; and the "body of death" becomes "life dominated by sin and death." While each individual choice can be defended, the cumulative effect is the loss of Paul's precise anthropological and theological vocabulary.

EMET and LOGOS agree more closely in this chapter than in previous ones, likely because chapter 7's first-person confessional style constrains the range of rendering options. The main divergences between them are stylistic (EMET's woodenness vs. LOGOS's naturalness) rather than theological.

The most significant translation decision is the consistent rendering of sarx as "flesh" rather than "sinful nature." This affects vv.5, 14, 18, and 25 and maintains the integrity of Paul's complex anthropological term, which in different contexts can mean the physical body, human nature, or the self under sin's dominion.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
